steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student claims IGF-1 gene variations 'must account for' size differences among dog breeds. The question asks which finding, if true, would most directly weaken this causal claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "The gene that regulates IGF-1 production is one of multiple genes known to affect the size of dogs.": If other genes also drive size differences, IGF-1 alone cannot account for all the variation. This directly undermines the claim. ✓
  • Choice B — "In fact, only one-third of the most prevalent dog breeds carry the same variant of the gene regulating IGF-1 production.": This actually supports the idea that IGF-1 variation correlates with size — it does not weaken the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — "Variations of the IGF-1 gene result in substantial body size variance among dog breeds and may influence additional characteristics as well.": This confirms the student's claim rather than challenging it. ✗
  • Choice D — "The researchers' conclusions regarding the IGF-1 gene may not apply to other species given that dogs vary in size more than any other group of land mammals does.": Scope across species is irrelevant to whether IGF-1 alone explains dog size. ✗
That is the correct answer.
